enrichment.short_description: Novu is an open-source notification infrastructure platform that lets developers trigger multi-channel notifications (email, SMS, push, chat and in-app) from a single API call and manage them with a visual workflow editor.
enrichment.seo_title: Novu Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Novu review 2026: open-source notification infrastructure for developers. See features, workflow-run pricing, integrations, pros, cons and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Novu is an open-source notification infrastructure platform for developers, founded in 2021 in Tel Aviv, Israel (originally launched as Notifire) by a small founding team that had repeatedly built and rebuilt in-house notification systems at previous companies. The company raised a $6.6 million seed round led by Crane Venture Partners, with participation from Eniac, MXV Capital, Entree Capital and angel investors including Ariel Maislos, to build a unified layer that lets engineering teams manage multi-channel messaging (email, SMS, push, chat and in-app inbox) through one API instead of stitching together separate provider SDKs for Twilio, SendGrid, Firebase, Slack and others. Its core open-source repository has become one of the most-starred notification projects on GitHub, and the hosted Novu Cloud sits on top of the same open-source workflow engine, so teams can self-host for free or pay for managed infrastructure. The product centers on a drag-and-drop workflow builder where developers define trigger conditions, channel fallbacks, digesting and batching logic, delay steps and in-app inbox rendering, then call a single trigger event from application code; Novu resolves which channels and providers to use per subscriber based on their preferences. It ships an embeddable Inbox component (React, Angular, Vue, and a headless API) for in-app notification centers, more than 55 provider integrations across email, SMS, push and chat, subscriber preference management, and multi-environment support for staging versus production workflows. Pricing is usage-based on workflow runs (one run equals one trigger event that can fan out to multiple channels): a Free tier includes 10,000 monthly workflow runs, up to 20 workflows, 2 environments and 3 team members; Pro starts at $30/month for 30,000+ runs, 7-day activity retention and no Novu branding; Team starts at $250/month for 250,000+ runs, up to 10 environments, unlimited members and role-based access control; Enterprise is custom-priced for 10 million-plus runs with HIPAA BAA, SSO, SCIM, private support and self-hosting options. Novu's target audience is product and platform engineering teams -- at startups through mid-market SaaS companies -- who need reliable, auditable notification delivery without building and maintaining bespoke messaging infrastructure. Its primary competitors include Courier, Knock, Suprsend and in-house solutions built directly on provider APIs like Twilio and SendGrid; Novu differentiates through its open-source core, generous self-hosting option, and developer-first workflow-as-code approach.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Novu is an open-source notification infrastructure platform built for developers who need to send email, SMS, push, chat and in-app notifications without wiring together separate provider SDKs. Instead of hard-coding logic for Twilio, SendGrid, Firebase Cloud Messaging and Slack directly into an application, teams define reusable workflows in Novu and trigger them with a single API call.

Founded in 2021 in Tel Aviv and originally launched under the name Notifire, Novu grew out of its founders' repeated frustration with rebuilding notification systems at previous jobs. The company raised a $6.6 million seed round and has since built both an open-source engine that any team can self-host and a managed cloud offering (Novu Cloud) for teams that would rather not run the infrastructure themselves.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: At the center of Novu is a visual workflow editor where developers configure trigger conditions, channel ordering and fallback logic, digest and batching windows, delay steps, and per-subscriber preferences, all version-controlled and testable across environments.

Novu also ships an embeddable Inbox component with SDKs for React, Angular and Vue (plus a headless API for any framework), giving product teams a ready-made in-app notification center. It supports more than 55 provider integrations spanning email, SMS, push and chat channels, along with subscriber preference centers, translation and localization support, and multi-environment workspaces so staging and production workflows stay isolated.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Novu prices its hosted cloud plans by monthly workflow runs, where one run corresponds to a single trigger event that can still deliver across several channels. The Free plan covers 10,000 runs per month with up to 20 workflows and 3 team members, making it usable for small projects and early-stage products.

Paid tiers scale from Pro at $30/month (30,000-plus runs, no Novu branding, longer activity retention) to Team at $250/month (250,000-plus runs, unlimited members, role-based access control) up to a custom-priced Enterprise plan with HIPAA support, SSO, SCIM and self-hosting for teams that need to run Novu on their own infrastructure at no per-run cost.

features.title: Visual workflow builder
features.description: Design multi-step, multi-channel notification workflows with trigger conditions, delays, digesting and fallback logic through a drag-and-drop editor.

features.title: Single trigger API
features.description: Fire one API call from application code and let Novu resolve the right channels, providers and content per subscriber.

features.title: Embeddable Inbox
features.description: Drop a pre-built in-app notification center into React, Angular or Vue apps, or build a custom one against the headless API.

features.title: 55-plus provider integrations
features.description: Connect existing email, SMS, push and chat providers such as SendGrid, Twilio, Firebase and Slack without rewriting delivery logic.

features.title: Digest and batching
features.description: Aggregate multiple events into a single digest notification to avoid overwhelming end users.

features.title: Subscriber preferences
features.description: Let end users opt in or out of specific notification types and channels through a preference center.

features.title: Multi-environment support
features.description: Separate development, staging and production workflows so testing never touches live subscribers.

features.title: Self-hosting option
features.description: Run the fully open-source engine on your own infrastructure to avoid per-run cloud pricing and keep full data control.

pros: Open-source core means no vendor lock-in and the option to self-host for free
Single API replaces integrations with multiple separate notification providers
Generous free tier (10,000 workflow runs/month) suitable for small projects
Visual workflow editor speeds up building and iterating on notification logic without redeploys
Embeddable Inbox component saves significant frontend engineering time for in-app notifications
cons: Usage-based workflow-run pricing can escalate quickly for high-volume applications
Team and Enterprise plans are priced well above typical small-team notification budgets
Self-hosting requires ongoing infrastructure maintenance that the cloud plan is meant to remove
Advanced features like SSO, SCIM and HIPAA support are locked behind custom Enterprise pricing
As a relatively young company (founded 2021), long-term roadmap stability is less proven than legacy messaging vendors

faqs.question: What is Novu used for?
faqs.answer: Novu is used by developers to build and manage multi-channel notification systems, sending email, SMS, push, chat and in-app notifications through one API and one workflow engine instead of separate provider integrations.

faqs.question: Is Novu free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Novu's open-source engine can be self-hosted for free, and the hosted Novu Cloud has a free tier that includes 10,000 workflow runs per month, all channels, and up to 3 team members.

faqs.question: Can Novu be self-hosted?
faqs.answer: Yes, Novu's core notification engine is open source and can be deployed on your own infrastructure, which avoids per-workflow-run cloud pricing entirely.

faqs.question: How does Novu's pricing work?
faqs.answer: Novu Cloud charges based on monthly workflow runs, where one run equals one trigger event that can fan out across multiple channels. Plans range from a free tier to Pro ($30/month), Team ($250/month) and custom Enterprise pricing.

faqs.question: What channels does Novu support?
faqs.answer: Novu supports email, SMS, push notifications, in-app inbox notifications and chat channels like Slack and Discord, through more than 55 built-in provider integrations.

faqs.question: Who is Novu built for?
faqs.answer: Novu is built for engineering and product teams that need reliable, centralized notification infrastructure, from early-stage startups to mid-market SaaS companies replacing custom-built notification systems.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Novu?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Courier, Knock and Suprsend, as well as building notification logic directly on top of individual provider APIs such as Twilio or SendGrid.

use_cases.title: Product notification centers
use_cases.description: Add an in-app notification inbox to a SaaS product using Novu's embeddable Inbox component instead of building one from scratch.

use_cases.title: Transactional and lifecycle messaging
use_cases.description: Trigger onboarding emails, password resets, billing alerts and usage digests across channels from unified workflows.

use_cases.title: Replacing fragmented provider integrations
use_cases.description: Consolidate separate SendGrid, Twilio and push-notification integrations into a single API and workflow layer to reduce maintenance overhead.

enrichment.short_description: nTask is an all-in-one project management platform combining task management, Kanban boards, time tracking, risk management and team collaboration for small and mid-sized teams.
enrichment.seo_title: nTask Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: nTask review 2026: all-in-one project management software with tasks, Kanban boards, time tracking and risk management. Compare pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: nTask is an all-in-one project and work management platform founded in 2015 by Fawad Ansari, now headquartered in Frisco, Texas, that positions itself as a simplified alternative to heavier enterprise project management tools. The platform is used by an estimated 200,000-plus teams globally for managing tasks, sprints, product roadmaps, client work and company goals from a single workspace. Its core feature set covers task and project management with list, board, Gantt and calendar views; Kanban-style boards for visual workflow tracking; built-in time tracking and timesheets; meeting management with agendas and minutes; issue and risk management modules aimed at teams that need lightweight governance without adopting a separate PMO tool; and team collaboration features including comments, file attachments and status updates. nTask differentiates itself from competitors like Asana, Trello, Monday.com and ClickUp primarily on price and on including risk and issue tracking natively rather than as an add-on, which appeals to teams in regulated or process-heavy industries such as IT services, engineering and consulting who need basic risk registers without enterprise PMO software. Pricing follows a freemium, per-user model: the Free plan supports up to 5 team members, 5 workspaces and 200 MB of storage forever at no cost; the Premium plan starts at $3 per user per month (billed annually) and adds unlimited projects and Kanban boards on a pay-as-you-grow basis; the Business plan is $8 per user per month (billed annually) and layers in risk management, custom statuses, custom roles and permissions, and other advanced administrative controls; and an Enterprise tier offers custom pricing with enterprise-grade security and deeper customization for larger organizations, available on request through nTask's sales team. The company is a small, privately held software business (estimated at roughly 10-15 employees) with modest but stable revenue, making it a lean, founder-run alternative to venture-backed project management incumbents. nTask's target audience is small to mid-sized teams and agencies, particularly cost-sensitive teams that want task management, time tracking and lightweight risk/issue tracking bundled together rather than purchased as separate tools, and organizations that find larger platforms like Monday.com, Asana or Wrike overly complex or expensive for their team size.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: nTask is an all-in-one project management platform founded in 2015 and headquartered in Frisco, Texas. It was built to give small and mid-sized teams a single workspace for tasks, projects, meetings, time tracking and basic risk management, without requiring separate tools for each function.

The platform is used by an estimated 200,000-plus organizations for managing campaigns, sprints, product development and client work, and is frequently positioned as a lower-cost, simpler alternative to larger enterprise project management suites.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: nTask combines task management with list, Kanban board, Gantt chart and calendar views, letting teams choose how they visualize work. It also includes built-in time tracking and timesheets, meeting management with agendas and shareable minutes, and file and document collaboration.

A standout feature relative to many task-management competitors is native issue and risk management, giving teams a lightweight risk register and issue log without needing a separate governance tool. Custom statuses, roles and permissions are available on higher-tier plans for teams that need more structured workflows.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: nTask uses a freemium, per-user pricing model. The Free plan allows up to 5 team members, 5 workspaces and 200 MB of storage indefinitely at no cost, making it accessible for very small teams.

Paid plans scale by user count: Premium starts at $3 per user per month (billed annually) with unlimited projects and boards, and Business is $8 per user per month (billed annually) adding risk management, custom roles and advanced permissions. An Enterprise tier with custom security and configuration is available by contacting nTask sales.

features.title: Task and project management
features.description: Organize work in list, Kanban board, Gantt chart and calendar views across unlimited projects on paid plans.

features.title: Time tracking and timesheets
features.description: Log billable and non-billable hours against tasks and projects for client billing and internal reporting.

features.title: Meeting management
features.description: Schedule meetings, share agendas, and record and distribute meeting minutes directly within projects.

features.title: Issue and risk management
features.description: Track project risks and issues with a built-in risk register, useful for teams that need lightweight governance.

features.title: Kanban boards
features.description: Visualize workflow stages and move tasks across custom board columns for agile-style task tracking.

features.title: Custom roles and permissions
features.description: Define granular access controls and custom statuses on Business and Enterprise plans.

features.title: Team collaboration
features.description: Comment on tasks, attach files, and get status updates in a shared workspace.

pros: Low per-user pricing makes it accessible for small teams and startups
Combines task management, time tracking and risk management in one tool
Free plan is usable indefinitely for very small teams
Multiple work views (list, Kanban, Gantt, calendar) suit different team preferences
Native issue and risk tracking is uncommon at this price point
cons: Interface and feature depth lag behind larger competitors like Asana or Monday.com
Free plan is capped at 5 team members and limited storage
Risk management features are only available on Business plan and above
Smaller company size means a narrower support and integration ecosystem than venture-backed rivals
Some advanced customization requires contacting sales on the Enterprise tier

faqs.question: What is nTask used for?
faqs.answer: nTask is used for project and task management, including planning, Kanban boards, time tracking, meeting management, and issue and risk tracking for small to mid-sized teams.

faqs.question: Is nTask free?
faqs.answer: Yes, nTask offers a free plan for up to 5 team members with 5 workspaces and 200 MB of storage, usable indefinitely without payment.

faqs.question: How much does nTask cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at $3 per user per month (billed annually) for Premium, and $8 per user per month (billed annually) for Business, which adds risk management and advanced permissions. Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Does nTask include time tracking?
faqs.answer: Yes, nTask has built-in time tracking and timesheets that can be logged against tasks and projects for reporting and billing.

faqs.question: Does nTask support risk management?
faqs.answer: Yes, nTask includes a native risk and issue management module on its Business and Enterprise plans, which is uncommon among similarly priced project management tools.

faqs.question: Who founded nTask and when?
faqs.answer: nTask was founded in 2015 by Fawad Ansari and is headquartered in Frisco, Texas.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to nTask?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Asana, Trello, Monday.com, ClickUp and Wrike, though nTask is typically positioned as a lower-cost option for smaller teams.

use_cases.title: Small team project tracking
use_cases.description: Manage tasks, deadlines and deliverables across multiple client or internal projects in one workspace.

use_cases.title: Agency and consulting time billing
use_cases.description: Track billable hours against client projects using built-in time tracking and timesheets.

use_cases.title: Lightweight project risk tracking
use_cases.description: Maintain a basic risk and issue register alongside day-to-day task management without adopting separate PMO software.

enrichment.short_description: ntfy is a simple, open-source HTTP-based pub-sub push notification service that lets developers and self-hosters send notifications to phones, desktops and scripts using basic PUT or POST requests.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (self-hosted or public server); hosted plans from $5/month
enrichment.seo_title: ntfy Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: ntfy review 2026: open-source push notifications via simple PUT/POST requests. Self-host free or use the hosted service. See features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: ntfy (pronounced 'notify') is a free and open-source, HTTP-based publish-subscribe notification service created by developer Philipp Heckel (GitHub handle binwiederhier) that lets anyone send push notifications to a phone, desktop or script with a single PUT or POST request, no account, SDK or app registration required. Topics are created on the fly simply by publishing or subscribing to a topic name, which acts as a lightweight, semi-private channel; subscribers receive messages instantly on iOS, Android, desktop browsers and via the command line. The project is licensed under Apache 2.0 and GPLv2, has grown to over 30,000 GitHub stars and 300-plus contributors, and ships frequent releases, making it a cornerstone of the self-hosting and homelab community as well as a popular choice for developer automation, CI/CD alerts, monitoring notifications and IoT/home-automation triggers. ntfy integrates natively with tools like GitHub Actions, Home Assistant, Sonarr, Radarr, Uptime Kuma and dozens of other self-hosted and DevOps tools, and supports rich notification features including priorities, action buttons, tags, emoji, file attachments and delivery scheduling. Because it is fully open source, anyone can self-host their own ntfy server at zero cost, retaining complete control over data and message topics. For users who do not want to run their own server, the project also operates a free public server at ntfy.sh (usable without signup, though topics are only semi-private since anyone who knows or guesses the topic name can subscribe) as well as a paid hosted tier that funds ongoing development: the Supporter plan is about $5-6/month with higher daily message limits, reserved (fully private) topics and larger attachment sizes; Pro is about $10-12/month with substantially higher throughput; and Business is about $20-25/month for teams needing the highest limits, with larger dedicated deployments available on request. ntfy's target users are developers, DevOps engineers, and self-hosting hobbyists who want simple, scriptable push notifications without the overhead of building a full notification pipeline or paying for a heavyweight commercial platform like Pushover or PagerDuty. Its main differentiator versus commercial alternatives like Pushover, Pushbullet or OneSignal is that the entire stack is open source and self-hostable, giving privacy-conscious and cost-conscious users a genuine free option rather than a limited trial.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: ntfy is an open-source, HTTP-based pub-sub notification service created by developer Philipp Heckel that grew from a personal project into a widely used tool in the self-hosting and developer communities. It lets anyone send a push notification to a phone, desktop or terminal with a simple PUT or POST request, without needing an account, API key or app registration.

Because ntfy is fully open source under Apache 2.0 and GPLv2, it can be self-hosted for complete control and zero cost, or used via the free public server at ntfy.sh. The project has attracted a large open-source community, with more than 30,000 GitHub stars and 300-plus contributors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Topics in ntfy are created on the fly simply by publishing or subscribing to a topic name, and messages support rich formatting including priorities, action buttons, tags, emoji shortcuts and file attachments. Native apps are available for iOS, Android and desktop, alongside a web app and simple curl-based publishing.

ntfy integrates with a wide range of DevOps and self-hosted tools, including GitHub Actions, Home Assistant, Uptime Kuma, Sonarr and Radarr, making it a popular choice for build alerts, monitoring notifications and home-automation triggers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: ntfy is free to use in two ways: self-hosting the open-source server on your own infrastructure at no cost, or using the public ntfy.sh server without signing up (topics act as a shared secret and are only semi-private).

For users who want private reserved topics, higher message limits and larger attachments without running their own server, ntfy.sh offers paid hosted tiers: Supporter (around $5-6/month), Pro (around $10-12/month) and Business (around $20-25/month), with larger custom deployments available on request.

features.title: Simple PUT/POST publishing
features.description: Send a notification with a single HTTP request from curl, scripts, CI pipelines or any language with an HTTP client.

features.title: On-the-fly topics
features.description: Create pub-sub channels instantly by publishing or subscribing to any topic name, with no pre-registration required.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Receive notifications on iOS, Android, desktop browsers and via the command line.

features.title: Self-hosting
features.description: Run your own ntfy server for full control over data, topics and retention at zero licensing cost.

features.title: Rich notification options
features.description: Attach files up to plan limits, set priority levels, add action buttons, tags and emoji shortcuts.

features.title: Broad automation integrations
features.description: Works out of the box with GitHub Actions, Home Assistant, Uptime Kuma, Sonarr, Radarr and other DevOps and home-lab tools.

features.title: Reserved private topics
features.description: Paid hosted plans allow reserving topics that are not guessable or shared, unlike the semi-private default topics.

pros: Fully open source and free to self-host with no feature restrictions
Extremely simple API, sending a notification requires only one HTTP request
No account or app registration needed to start using the public server
Strong integration support across DevOps, monitoring and home-automation tools
Active open-source community with frequent releases and transparent development
cons: Default public-server topics are only semi-private, not fully secure by design
Self-hosting requires basic server administration knowledge
Paid hosted tiers are needed for reserved private topics and higher throughput
Lacks the polished enterprise features and SLAs of commercial alerting platforms like PagerDuty
Attachment size and message-rate limits on the free public server can be restrictive for heavy use

faqs.question: What is ntfy used for?
faqs.answer: ntfy is used to send push notifications to phones, desktops or scripts via simple HTTP requests, commonly for CI/CD alerts, server monitoring, home-automation triggers and personal reminders.

faqs.question: Is ntfy free?
faqs.answer: Yes, ntfy is free and open source. You can self-host your own server at no cost or use the public ntfy.sh server without an account.

faqs.question: Can I self-host ntfy?
faqs.answer: Yes, ntfy's server is fully open source under Apache 2.0 and GPLv2 and is designed to be self-hosted on your own infrastructure.

faqs.question: How much do ntfy's paid plans cost?
faqs.answer: Hosted plans on ntfy.sh start around $5-6/month for Supporter, scaling to around $10-12/month for Pro and $20-25/month for Business, offering higher limits and reserved private topics.

faqs.question: Who created ntfy?
faqs.answer: ntfy was created by developer Philipp Heckel, known on GitHub as binwiederhier, and has since grown into a community-driven open-source project.

faqs.question: Is ntfy secure and private?
faqs.answer: By default, public server topics act like a shared password and are only semi-private. For stronger privacy, users can self-host or subscribe to a paid plan with reserved, non-guessable topics.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to ntfy?
faqs.answer: Alternatives include Pushover, Pushbullet, Gotify and OneSignal, though ntfy is distinguished by being fully open source and self-hostable at no cost.

use_cases.title: CI/CD and server monitoring alerts
use_cases.description: Trigger push notifications from build pipelines, cron jobs or uptime monitors when a job fails or a server goes down.

use_cases.title: Home lab and home automation notifications
use_cases.description: Send alerts from Home Assistant, Sonarr, Radarr or other self-hosted services directly to a phone.

use_cases.title: Lightweight personal or team alerting
use_cases.description: Replace a heavyweight paid notification service with a simple, scriptable HTTP-based alerting system.

features: Public ntfy.sh server, no signup required

enrichment.short_description: Nuclei is a fast, open-source, template-based vulnerability scanner from ProjectDiscovery that uses a YAML DSL to detect security issues across web applications, APIs, networks, DNS and cloud configurations.
enrichment.starting_price: Free (open source); ProjectDiscovery Cloud Platform pricing is custom
enrichment.seo_title: Nuclei Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Nuclei review 2026: ProjectDiscovery's open-source, template-based vulnerability scanner. See features, YAML DSL scanning, pricing and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Nuclei is an open-source, template-based vulnerability scanner built by ProjectDiscovery, a cybersecurity company founded in 2019 by Sandeep Singh, Rishiraj Sharma, Marco Rivoli and Nizamul Rana and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Rather than relying on hardcoded, general-purpose scanning logic, Nuclei runs against a target using thousands of community-authored and officially maintained YAML-based templates, each expressing a specific vulnerability check, misconfiguration signature or exposure pattern, spanning web application vulnerabilities, exposed panels and default credentials, DNS and network issues, API weaknesses, cloud misconfigurations, and CVEs disclosed for popular software. Because checks are defined declaratively in a simple domain-specific language rather than compiled code, the security community can write, share and update templates rapidly, which is why Nuclei's public template repository grows continuously to cover newly disclosed CVEs, often within hours of public disclosure. Nuclei is written in Go for speed and low resource usage, supports massively parallel scanning across large target lists, and integrates cleanly into CI/CD pipelines, bug bounty workflows and continuous attack-surface monitoring programs. It sees more than 50 million scans per month from security researchers, penetration testers and engineering teams in over 100 countries, and is one of the most widely adopted open-source vulnerability scanners in the security community, alongside tools like Nmap, OpenVAS and Burp Suite. Nuclei itself is completely free and open source under an MIT-style license with no feature paywall; ProjectDiscovery instead monetizes through a separate commercial product, the ProjectDiscovery Cloud Platform, which layers continuous attack-surface management, hosted scanning infrastructure, team collaboration and reporting on top of the open-source Nuclei engine at custom, sales-quoted pricing. ProjectDiscovery has raised a total of roughly $25 million, including a $25 million Series A round in 2023 led by CRV with participation from Point72 Ventures, SignalFire, Accel and Lightspeed, among others. Nuclei's target users are penetration testers, red teams, bug bounty hunters, application security engineers and DevSecOps teams who need fast, automatable, continuously updated vulnerability detection that plugs directly into existing security tooling and CI/CD workflows, rather than a closed-source commercial scanner with a fixed vulnerability database.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Nuclei is an open-source vulnerability scanner developed by ProjectDiscovery, a San Francisco-based cybersecurity company founded in 2019. Unlike traditional scanners with hardcoded detection logic, Nuclei runs configurable, community-authored templates written in a simple YAML-based domain-specific language, letting the security community add and update checks continuously.

The project has become one of the most widely used open-source scanners in security testing, processing over 50 million scans a month across more than 100 countries, and is frequently used in penetration testing, bug bounty hunting and continuous attack-surface monitoring.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Nuclei's core strength is its template-based architecture: each vulnerability check, misconfiguration or exposure signature is defined declaratively in YAML rather than compiled into the tool itself, so new checks for freshly disclosed CVEs can ship within hours through the public template repository.

Built in Go for speed, Nuclei supports high-concurrency scanning across large target lists and covers web applications, APIs, DNS, network services and cloud misconfigurations. It is designed to be scripted into CI/CD pipelines and automation workflows rather than run only as a manual, interactive tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Nuclei itself is completely free and open source, with no paywalled features or usage limits when self-hosted and run from the command line or CI pipeline.

ProjectDiscovery monetizes separately through the ProjectDiscovery Cloud Platform, a hosted product built on top of Nuclei that adds continuous attack-surface management, team collaboration and reporting; pricing for that platform is custom and quoted directly by ProjectDiscovery's sales team.

features.title: Template-based scanning engine
features.description: Detect vulnerabilities using a YAML DSL where each check is a shareable, version-controlled template rather than hardcoded logic.

features.title: Massive community template library
features.description: Access thousands of officially maintained and community-contributed templates covering CVEs, misconfigurations and exposures.

features.title: Fast, concurrent scanning
features.description: Written in Go for high-throughput scanning across large target lists with configurable rate limiting.

features.title: Multi-protocol coverage
features.description: Scan web applications, APIs, DNS records, network services and cloud configurations from a single tool.

features.title: CI/CD and automation integration
features.description: Run Nuclei as part of build pipelines and security automation workflows for continuous vulnerability detection.

features.title: Rapid CVE coverage
features.description: New templates for high-profile disclosed vulnerabilities are frequently published within hours of public disclosure.

features.title: Custom template authoring
features.description: Write organization-specific templates to detect proprietary application logic flaws or internal misconfigurations.

pros: Completely free and open source with no feature restrictions
Extremely fast, low-overhead scanning suitable for large-scale and CI/CD use
Massive, continuously updated community template library covers new CVEs quickly
Declarative YAML templates are easy to read, write and audit compared to compiled scanner logic
Backed by an active, well-funded company (ProjectDiscovery) ensuring ongoing development
cons: Effectiveness depends heavily on template coverage and quality, gaps can mean missed vulnerabilities
Command-line-first tool has a steeper learning curve than GUI-based commercial scanners
No built-in reporting, dashboards or team collaboration, those require the separate paid Cloud Platform
False positives can occur with community-submitted templates that are not officially vetted
Requires ongoing template updates and tuning to stay effective against new threats

faqs.question: What is Nuclei used for?
faqs.answer: Nuclei is used to scan web applications, APIs, networks, DNS and cloud configurations for known vulnerabilities, misconfigurations and exposures using community-maintained YAML templates.

faqs.question: Is Nuclei free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nuclei is completely free and open source with no paywalled features. ProjectDiscovery separately sells a hosted Cloud Platform built on top of it.

faqs.question: Who makes Nuclei?
faqs.answer: Nuclei is developed by ProjectDiscovery, a cybersecurity company founded in 2019 and headquartered in San Francisco, which has raised roughly $25 million in funding including a Series A led by CRV.

faqs.question: How does Nuclei detect vulnerabilities?
faqs.answer: Nuclei runs configurable templates written in a YAML-based domain-specific language against a target, each template encoding a specific vulnerability check, misconfiguration signature or exposure pattern.

faqs.question: Is Nuclei suitable for CI/CD pipelines?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nuclei is commonly integrated into CI/CD pipelines and automated security workflows to continuously scan for newly disclosed vulnerabilities.

faqs.question: Who typically uses Nuclei?
faqs.answer: Penetration testers, bug bounty hunters, red teams and DevSecOps engineers use Nuclei for fast, automatable vulnerability scanning.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Nuclei?
faqs.answer: Alternatives include Nmap with NSE scripts, OpenVAS, Burp Suite and Nessus, though Nuclei is distinguished by its lightweight YAML template model and open-source, community-driven update cadence.

use_cases.title: Continuous vulnerability scanning in CI/CD
use_cases.description: Run Nuclei automatically on every deployment or on a schedule to catch newly introduced or newly disclosed vulnerabilities early.

use_cases.title: Penetration testing and bug bounty reconnaissance
use_cases.description: Quickly identify known vulnerabilities and misconfigurations across large sets of targets during engagements.
use_cases.audience: Penetration testers and bug bounty hunters

use_cases.title: Attack surface monitoring
use_cases.description: Continuously scan external-facing assets for exposures and CVEs as part of an ongoing security posture program.

enrichment.short_description: Nuclino is a fast, real-time team wiki and knowledge base that lets teams create, organize and collaboratively edit documents, notes and boards in a single lightweight workspace.
enrichment.seo_title: Nuclino Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Nuclino review 2026: the fast, real-time team wiki for docs and knowledge bases. Compare features, pricing plans and top Nuclino alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Nuclino is a real-time collaborative team wiki and knowledge base platform founded in 2015 near Munich, Germany by co-founders Bjorn Michelsen (CEO), Jonathan Kienzle (CTO) and Veronika Fischer (COO), and now headquartered in Grunwald, Germany. The company is notable for being fully bootstrapped and profitable, having raised no external venture funding while growing to over $3 million in annual recurring revenue with a lean team of roughly 20 people, and reporting around $598,000 in 2024 revenue on a platform serving over 12,000 customers. Nuclino's core pitch is speed and simplicity: it is marketed as 'your team's collective brain,' a lightweight internal wiki that loads instantly, avoids the feature bloat of larger tools like Notion or Confluence, and lets teams organize documentation, meeting notes, product specs and onboarding material in nested, interlinked items. Content is organized into workspaces containing collections of items that can be viewed as a list, board (Kanban-style), grid or graph view showing how documents link to one another, and editing happens in real time with Google Docs-style multiplayer cursors. Nuclino supports rich text editing, embedded files, code blocks and diagrams, deep linking between items to build a wiki-style knowledge graph, and integrations with tools like Slack, Figma and Google Drive for embedding external content directly into pages. Pricing follows a freemium SaaS model: the Free plan supports up to 50 items and 2 GB of total storage, suitable for individuals or very small teams evaluating the product; the Starter plan is priced at roughly $6 per user per month billed annually (around $8/month billed monthly) and unlocks unlimited items and boards along with more integrations; the Business plan is around $10 per user per month and adds AI-powered features, advanced permissions, audit logs and enterprise-grade security controls; and an Enterprise tier is available at custom pricing for larger organizations with specific compliance or deployment needs. Nuclino's target audience is small to mid-sized teams -- particularly startups, agencies and remote-first companies -- that want a fast, distraction-free internal wiki without the complexity, cost or steep learning curve of Notion, Confluence or Coda. Its primary competitors include Notion, Confluence, Slite, Coda and Guru, with Nuclino differentiating itself on raw speed, minimalism and its unique graph view of interlinked documents.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Nuclino is a real-time team wiki and knowledge base founded in 2015 near Munich, Germany, and now based in Grunwald. Unlike most of its venture-backed competitors, Nuclino has remained fully bootstrapped and profitable since founding, growing to more than $3 million in annual recurring revenue and over 12,000 customers with a small team of around 20 people.

The product is built around the idea of being 'your team's collective brain,' emphasizing instant load times and a minimal interface over the sprawling feature sets found in tools like Notion or Confluence.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Teams organize content into workspaces made up of interlinked items, which can be viewed as a list, Kanban-style board, grid, or a unique graph view that visually maps how documents connect to one another. Editing is real time and multiplayer, similar to Google Docs, with rich text, embedded files, code blocks and diagram support.

Nuclino integrates with tools such as Slack, Figma and Google Drive to embed external content directly into wiki pages, and higher-tier plans add AI-assisted writing tools, advanced permissions and audit logs for larger organizations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Nuclino's Free plan covers up to 50 items and 2 GB of storage, enough for individuals or very small teams to fully evaluate the product before paying.

Paid plans are per-user: Starter runs about $6/user/month billed annually (around $8/month billed monthly) with unlimited items and boards, Business runs about $10/user/month and adds AI features, advanced permissions and audit logs, and Enterprise is custom-priced for larger deployments.

features.title: Real-time collaborative editing
features.description: Multiple team members can edit the same document simultaneously with live multiplayer cursors, similar to Google Docs.

features.title: Graph view
features.description: Visualize how wiki items link to one another in an interactive graph, making it easy to discover related documentation.

features.title: Multiple content views
features.description: Switch between list, Kanban-style board and grid views to organize the same underlying content differently.

features.title: Instant search and fast performance
features.description: Nuclino is built for near-instant load times and search results even in large knowledge bases.

features.title: Rich content embedding
features.description: Embed files, images, code blocks, diagrams and content from tools like Figma and Google Drive directly into pages.

features.title: Nested, interlinked items
features.description: Structure documentation hierarchically while cross-linking related items to build a true wiki-style knowledge graph.

features.title: Advanced permissions and audit logs
features.description: Business and Enterprise plans add granular access controls and activity auditing for larger teams.

pros: Noticeably faster and simpler than heavier tools like Notion or Confluence
Free plan is generous enough for small teams to fully evaluate before paying
Unique graph view makes it easy to see how documentation is interconnected
Bootstrapped, profitable company with a stable, long-running product (founded 2015)
Real-time multiplayer editing works smoothly even with several simultaneous editors
cons: Fewer advanced features (databases, automations, integrations) compared to Notion or Coda
Free plan's 50-item cap is restrictive for growing teams
AI features and audit logs are locked behind the Business tier
Smaller company size than VC-backed competitors means a slower pace of major feature releases
Less extensive third-party integration ecosystem than larger knowledge-base platforms

faqs.question: What is Nuclino used for?
faqs.answer: Nuclino is used as a team wiki and knowledge base for documenting processes, meeting notes, product specs and onboarding material in a fast, real-time collaborative workspace.

faqs.question: Is Nuclino free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nuclino offers a free plan for up to 50 items and 2 GB of storage, suitable for individuals or small teams.

faqs.question: How much does Nuclino cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at roughly $6 per user per month billed annually for the Starter plan, with the Business plan around $10 per user per month; Enterprise pricing is custom.

faqs.question: Is Nuclino a Notion alternative?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nuclino is commonly used as a faster, simpler alternative to Notion for teams that primarily need a wiki and knowledge base rather than databases and complex automations.

faqs.question: Who founded Nuclino?
faqs.answer: Nuclino was founded in 2015 near Munich, Germany by Bjorn Michelsen, Jonathan Kienzle and Veronika Fischer, and has remained self-funded and profitable ever since.

faqs.question: Does Nuclino support real-time collaboration?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nuclino supports real-time multiplayer editing where multiple team members can edit the same document simultaneously.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Nuclino?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Notion, Confluence, Slite, Coda and Guru, with Nuclino distinguished by its speed, minimalism and graph view of linked content.

enrichment.short_description: Nue is an omnichannel quote-to-revenue platform for B2B SaaS companies, unifying CPQ (configure-price-quote), subscription billing, revenue recognition and usage-based pricing in one system.
enrichment.seo_title: Nue Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Nue review 2026: omnichannel quote-to-revenue platform for B2B SaaS. See CPQ, billing and revenue features, pricing model and top alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Nue is a quote-to-revenue platform for B2B SaaS companies, originally founded in 2019 under the name Ruby.io before rebranding to Nue, and headquartered in San Francisco, California. The company has raised approximately $35 million in total funding across three rounds, including a $20 million Series A announced in early 2025 led by Inovia Capital with participation from Information Venture Partners, Bluefish Capital, NextWorld Capital, NJP Ventures and Operator Stack Fund, and has grown from roughly 41 employees in 2023 to around 120 employees by 2026, with reported annual revenue near $10 million. Nue positions itself as a modern, unified alternative to stitching together separate CPQ, billing and revenue-recognition point solutions (commonly Salesforce CPQ, Zuora and a separate revenue recognition tool), instead delivering configure-price-quote, subscription and usage-based billing, order management, and revenue intelligence on a single platform built natively on Salesforce and designed for the complexity of modern SaaS pricing models like seat-based, usage-based and hybrid pricing. Core capabilities include a Price Builder for deploying and testing pricing and promotions quickly, a quote and order management module that lets sales reps build complex, multi-product quotes and get customer proposals out significantly faster than legacy CPQ tools, flexible billing that supports usage-based, subscription and hybrid revenue models, and revenue intelligence dashboards that give finance teams real-time visibility into ARR, expansion and renewal metrics without manual reconciliation across systems. Nue integrates with e-signature and contract lifecycle tools including DocuSign, Ironclad, PandaDoc and Conga, and offers implementation programs, including a Quick Start track that aims to get customers from kickoff to production in as little as four to six weeks. Nue does not publish self-serve pricing; prospective customers request a live demo and receive a custom quote based on their revenue model, deal volume and existing tech stack, which is typical for enterprise-grade quote-to-cash software. Nue's target audience is mid-market and enterprise B2B SaaS companies -- particularly those with complex, evolving pricing models -- whose revenue operations, sales and finance teams have outgrown spreadsheets or a legacy CPQ tool like Salesforce CPQ. Its main competitors include Salesforce CPQ, Zuora, Chargebee, Maxio (formerly SaaSOptics/Chargify) and DealHub, with Nue differentiating through its unified quote-to-revenue architecture rather than requiring separate best-of-breed tools stitched together via integrations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Nue is a quote-to-revenue platform for B2B SaaS companies, founded in 2019 (originally as Ruby.io) and headquartered in San Francisco. It unifies configure-price-quote (CPQ), subscription and usage-based billing, order management and revenue intelligence into a single platform, replacing the common approach of running separate CPQ, billing and revenue-recognition tools.

The company has raised around $35 million in total funding, including a $20 million Series A led by Inovia Capital in 2025, and has grown to roughly 120 employees as it scales its customer base of mid-market and enterprise SaaS companies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Nue's Price Builder lets revenue teams design and deploy complex pricing models and promotions quickly, while its quoting engine helps sales reps assemble multi-product proposals and send them out substantially faster than with legacy CPQ tools.

On the finance side, Nue supports usage-based, subscription and hybrid billing models along with revenue intelligence dashboards that give real-time visibility into ARR, renewals and expansion revenue, removing the need for manual reconciliation across disconnected billing and CRM systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Nue does not publish public, self-serve pricing. As an enterprise quote-to-cash platform, pricing is quoted individually based on a company's revenue model, transaction volume, integrations needed and implementation scope.

Prospective customers typically request a live demo through Nue's website, after which the sales team scopes a custom package and implementation timeline, with fast-track onboarding programs available for teams needing to go live within a matter of weeks.

features.description: Quick Start onboarding aims to move customers from kickoff to live production in as little as four to six weeks.

pros: Unifies CPQ, billing and revenue intelligence in one platform instead of multiple point solutions
Purpose-built for complex, modern SaaS pricing models including usage-based and hybrid pricing
Native Salesforce integration keeps sales and revenue data synchronized
Well-funded ($35M raised) with an experienced RevOps-focused team backing continued development
Fast implementation track can bring customers live in weeks rather than months
cons: No public pricing, requires a sales conversation before understanding cost
Primarily built for Salesforce-centric organizations, less suited to non-Salesforce CRM stacks
Younger, smaller company than incumbents like Zuora or Salesforce CPQ, so ecosystem and track record are less established
Best suited to mid-market and enterprise companies, likely overkill for very small SaaS businesses
Migrating from legacy CPQ or billing systems can require a significant implementation effort

faqs.question: What is Nue used for?
faqs.answer: Nue is used by B2B SaaS companies to manage configure-price-quote (CPQ), subscription and usage-based billing, order management and revenue intelligence in one unified platform.

faqs.question: How much does Nue cost?
faqs.answer: Nue does not publish public pricing. Cost is quoted individually based on a company's revenue model, deal volume and implementation needs after a sales demo.

faqs.question: Is Nue built on Salesforce?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nue is built natively on the Salesforce platform, allowing quoting, billing and revenue data to stay synchronized with existing CRM workflows.

faqs.question: How much funding has Nue raised?

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Nue?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Salesforce CPQ, Zuora, Chargebee, Maxio and DealHub, with Nue differentiating through its unified quote-to-revenue architecture.

enrichment.short_description: Numeral is an AI-native sales tax compliance platform that automates nexus monitoring, tax calculation, registration, filing and remittance for e-commerce and SaaS businesses.
enrichment.starting_price: Custom (per-transaction pricing based on registrations and filings)
enrichment.seo_title: Numeral Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Numeral review 2026: AI-native sales tax automation for e-commerce and SaaS. See nexus monitoring, filing and remittance features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Numeral is an AI-native sales tax compliance platform founded in 2023 by Sam Ross (CEO) and Matt DuVall (CTO), former Stripe and Notion engineers, and headquartered in San Francisco, California. Backed by Y Combinator and investors including Mayfield, Benchmark, Uncork Capital and Mantis, Numeral has raised approximately $57 million in total funding, including a $35 million Series B announced in September 2025 led by Mayfield at a reported valuation near $350 million, and has grown to roughly 40-plus employees serving e-commerce and SaaS customers such as Brex, Character.AI and Fishwife. The platform targets a persistent pain point for growing online businesses: US sales tax compliance, which requires tracking economic nexus thresholds across thousands of state and local jurisdictions, registering for tax permits once thresholds are crossed, calculating correct tax at checkout, and filing and remitting returns on time, historically requiring either a manual, error-prone finance process or expensive managed services. Numeral automates the full lifecycle: its nexus monitoring engine tracks a company's transaction volume and taxable revenue against state-by-state economic nexus thresholds and automatically initiates registration when a threshold is crossed; its tax calculation API integrates with billing and e-commerce platforms such as Stripe, Shopify, Amazon and NetSuite to apply correct rates at the point of sale; and its filing and remittance engine prepares and submits returns to tax authorities on the company's behalf, backed by a guarantee that Numeral will cover penalties and interest if a filing is late due to its error. Additional capabilities include exemption certificate management for validating and storing customer tax-exemption documentation, virtual mailboxes that receive and digitize government correspondence on a company's behalf, and support for global VAT compliance across more than 80 countries for companies selling internationally. Numeral prices per transaction based on the number of registrations and filings a business needs, with no long-term contracts required, and includes white-glove customer support as part of its offering rather than as a paid add-on. Numeral's target audience is fast-growing e-commerce brands and SaaS companies that have crossed or are approaching economic nexus thresholds in multiple states and want to outsource sales tax operations entirely rather than hire an in-house tax team or manage the process through spreadsheets and a traditional tax software vendor. Its primary competitors include Avalara, TaxJar (by Stripe), Vertex and Anrok, with Numeral differentiating through its combination of AI-driven automation, a done-for-you filing guarantee and a modern, API-first integration approach aimed specifically at digital-first businesses.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Numeral is an AI-native sales tax compliance platform founded in 2023 by former Stripe and Notion engineers Sam Ross and Matt DuVall, and headquartered in San Francisco. The company went through Y Combinator and has since raised approximately $57 million in total funding, including a $35 million Series B led by Mayfield in 2025.

Numeral automates the full US sales tax lifecycle for e-commerce and SaaS businesses, from nexus monitoring and registration through tax calculation, filing and remittance, positioning itself as a done-for-you alternative to manual compliance processes or traditional tax software.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Numeral's nexus monitoring engine tracks transaction volume and revenue against each state's economic nexus thresholds and automatically triggers registration once a business crosses a threshold, removing the need for finance teams to track this manually across dozens of jurisdictions.

Its tax calculation API integrates with platforms like Stripe, Shopify, Amazon and NetSuite to apply correct sales tax at checkout, while its filing and remittance service prepares and submits returns on the customer's behalf, backed by a guarantee to cover penalties and interest for any late filing caused by Numeral's error.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Numeral prices per transaction based on the number of tax registrations and filings a business requires, rather than a flat monthly SaaS fee, and does not require long-term contracts.

Exact pricing depends on the number of states a business is registered in and its filing frequency, so prospective customers typically get a tailored quote after sharing their transaction volume and jurisdictional footprint.

features.title: Economic nexus monitoring
features.description: Automatically tracks transaction volume and revenue against state-by-state economic nexus thresholds.

features.title: Automated tax registration
features.description: Initiates state tax registration automatically once a nexus threshold is crossed.

features.title: Tax calculation API
features.description: Applies accurate sales tax at checkout through integrations with Stripe, Shopify, Amazon and NetSuite.

features.title: Filing and remittance with guarantee
features.description: Prepares and submits sales tax returns on time, with a guarantee to cover penalties and interest for Numeral's errors.

features.title: Exemption certificate management
features.description: Collects, validates and stores customer sales tax exemption certificates.

features.title: Virtual mailboxes
features.description: Receives and digitizes government tax correspondence on the business's behalf.

features.title: Global VAT support
features.description: Extends compliance coverage to more than 80 countries for internationally selling businesses.

pros: Fully automates a historically manual and error-prone compliance process end to end
Filing guarantee covers penalties and interest for errors caused by Numeral
Per-transaction pricing with no long-term contract required
Integrates directly with common billing and e-commerce platforms like Stripe and Shopify
Backed by significant recent funding ($57M total, including a 2025 Series B), suggesting strong ongoing investment in the product
cons: No public, self-serve pricing, businesses must get a custom quote
As a company founded in 2023, it has a shorter track record than legacy providers like Avalara
Primarily optimized for e-commerce and SaaS billing stacks, may be less suited to businesses on niche platforms
Global VAT support, while offered, is a newer capability compared to the platform's core US sales tax focus
Full automation requires accurate integration setup, mistakes in initial configuration could affect filings

faqs.question: What is Numeral used for?
faqs.answer: Numeral is used by e-commerce and SaaS businesses to automate US sales tax compliance, including nexus monitoring, registration, tax calculation, filing and remittance.

faqs.question: How much does Numeral cost?
faqs.answer: Numeral uses per-transaction pricing based on the number of tax registrations and filings needed, with no long-term contracts. Exact pricing requires a custom quote.

faqs.question: Who founded Numeral?
faqs.answer: Numeral was founded in 2023 by Sam Ross and Matt DuVall, former engineers at Stripe and Notion, and is headquartered in San Francisco.

faqs.question: Does Numeral guarantee on-time tax filing?
faqs.answer: Yes, Numeral guarantees that sales tax will be filed on time, or it will cover any resulting penalties and interest charges caused by its error.

faqs.question: What platforms does Numeral integrate with?
faqs.answer: Numeral integrates with billing and e-commerce platforms including Stripe, Shopify, Amazon and NetSuite, among others.

faqs.question: Does Numeral support international tax compliance?
faqs.answer: Yes, in addition to US sales tax, Numeral offers global VAT compliance support across more than 80 countries.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Numeral?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Avalara, TaxJar, Vertex and Anrok, with Numeral differentiating through its AI-driven, done-for-you approach and filing guarantee.

enrichment.short_description: Nutrium is cloud-based practice management and EHR software for nutritionists and dietitians, combining meal planning, telehealth, scheduling and client management in one platform.
enrichment.seo_title: Nutrium Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Nutrium review 2026: cloud-based practice management software for dietitians. See meal planning, telehealth and scheduling features, pricing and alternatives.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Nutrium is cloud-based practice management and electronic health record (EHR) software built specifically for nutritionists and dietitians, founded in 2015 in Braga, Portugal by Pedro Carneiro, Diogo Alves and Andre Santos. The company has raised roughly $19.8 million across four funding rounds and has grown to a team estimated between 90 and 190 employees depending on the source, positioning it as one of the largest dedicated software vendors serving the nutrition and dietetics profession globally, marketed as 'the world's number one nutrition software.' Nutrium's platform consolidates the tools a nutrition practice needs into one system: personalized meal plan creation with access to a library of 100-plus ready-to-use templates, recipes and client-facing infographics; automated appointment scheduling with reminders and follow-ups; secure in-app chat and telehealth video consultations for remote client sessions; dietary and nutrient assessment tools that let practitioners analyze a client's intake against nutritional targets; visual progress tracking so clients and practitioners can see trends over time; and built-in invoicing and payment collection so practitioners can run the business side of their practice without a separate billing tool. The platform also offers a client-facing mobile app that lets patients log meals, message their practitioner and view their meal plans between sessions, which is a key differentiator versus generic EHR or scheduling software not built for the nutrition workflow. Pricing is structured around client volume rather than feature tiers: both of Nutrium's plans include the full feature set, so a solo practitioner or dietitian starting out can choose the lower-priced plan capped at 10 clients per month (roughly $15/month billed annually, or $25/month billed monthly) and upgrade to the Unlimited Clients plan (roughly $25/month billed annually, or $39/month billed monthly) as their caseload grows, with all plans including a 14-day free trial and no permanently free tier. Nutrium's target audience is independent dietitians, nutritionists and small nutrition clinics, as well as larger healthcare organizations and corporate wellness programs that license Nutrium's software for their in-house nutrition teams (the company has separately expanded into workplace nutrition and employee benefits offerings). Its primary competitors include NutriAdmin, Healthie, Nutrium's category peers like Cronometer Professional and general practice-management tools adapted for nutrition use, with Nutrium differentiating through its purpose-built combination of meal planning, telehealth and practice management in a single platform designed specifically around the day-to-day workflow of a nutrition professional.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Nutrium is cloud-based practice management and EHR software purpose-built for nutritionists and dietitians, founded in 2015 in Braga, Portugal by Pedro Carneiro, Diogo Alves and Andre Santos. The company has raised approximately $19.8 million in funding and grown into one of the largest dedicated software providers serving the global nutrition and dietetics profession.

Rather than adapting generic scheduling or EHR software to nutrition workflows, Nutrium was designed from the ground up around the specific needs of a nutrition practice: meal planning, dietary analysis, client communication and the business operations that come with running a private practice.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Nutrium lets practitioners build personalized meal plans using a library of over 100 ready-to-use templates, recipes and client-facing infographics, and includes dietary assessment tools for analyzing nutrient intake against targets. Appointments can be scheduled automatically with reminders, and sessions can be conducted remotely through built-in secure chat and telehealth video.

A companion client mobile app lets patients log meals, message their practitioner and review their meal plans between appointments, while built-in invoicing and payment collection let practitioners manage billing without a separate tool.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Nutrium prices by client volume rather than by feature access, since both plans include the platform's full functionality. The lower-volume plan, capped at 10 clients per month, starts around $15/month billed annually (or $25/month billed monthly).

The Unlimited Clients plan starts around $25/month billed annually (or $39/month billed monthly) for practitioners with larger caseloads. There is no permanently free tier, but both plans include a 14-day free trial.

features.title: Personalized meal planning
features.description: Build custom meal plans for clients using a library of 100-plus templates, recipes and infographics.

features.title: Telehealth video consultations
features.description: Conduct remote client sessions directly within the platform with built-in secure video calling.

features.title: Automated scheduling and reminders
features.description: Manage appointment booking, reminders and follow-ups without manual coordination.

features.title: Dietary and nutrient assessment
features.description: Analyze client dietary intake against nutritional targets using built-in assessment tools.

features.title: Client mobile app
features.description: Give clients a dedicated app to log meals, view meal plans and message their practitioner between sessions.

features.title: Progress tracking
features.description: Visualize client progress over time with charts and reports shared between practitioner and client.

features.title: Invoicing and payments
features.description: Collect payments and manage billing for consultations directly within the platform.

pros: Purpose-built specifically for nutrition professionals rather than adapted from generic EHR software
Combines meal planning, telehealth, scheduling and billing in a single subscription
Client-facing mobile app improves engagement and communication between sessions
Both pricing tiers include the full feature set, so practitioners aren't forced to pay more just to unlock features
Well-funded and established company (founded 2015, $19.8M raised) with a large existing user base
cons: No permanently free plan, only a 14-day trial before payment is required
Pricing is tied to client volume, so costs rise as a practice grows past 10 clients on the lower tier
Monthly billing is notably more expensive than annual billing, which requires an upfront commitment for the best rate
Feature depth in areas like insurance billing may be more limited than region-specific competitors
As a practice-specific tool, it lacks broader general-purpose EHR capabilities some multi-specialty clinics may need

faqs.question: What is Nutrium used for?
faqs.answer: Nutrium is used by nutritionists and dietitians to manage their practice, including meal planning, telehealth consultations, appointment scheduling, dietary assessment and client billing.

faqs.question: Is Nutrium free?
faqs.answer: Nutrium does not offer a permanently free plan, but it does include a 14-day free trial on all plans before payment is required.

faqs.question: How much does Nutrium cost?
faqs.answer: Nutrium's lower-volume plan (up to 10 clients/month) starts around $15/month billed annually, and the Unlimited Clients plan starts around $25/month billed annually; monthly billing is priced higher.

faqs.question: Does Nutrium support telehealth?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nutrium includes built-in secure telehealth video consultations and in-app chat for remote client sessions.

faqs.question: Who founded Nutrium?
faqs.answer: Nutrium was founded in 2015 in Braga, Portugal by Pedro Carneiro, Diogo Alves and Andre Santos.

faqs.question: Does Nutrium have a client-facing app?
faqs.answer: Yes, Nutrium offers a mobile app for clients to log meals, view their meal plans and message their practitioner between appointments.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Nutrium?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include NutriAdmin, Healthie and Cronometer Professional, with Nutrium differentiating through its all-in-one combination of meal planning, telehealth and practice management.

use_cases.title: Solo dietitian private practice
use_cases.description: Run an entire nutrition practice, from scheduling and consultations to meal planning and billing, in one platform.

use_cases.title: Remote nutrition coaching
use_cases.description: Deliver telehealth consultations and ongoing meal plan support to clients who can't attend in person.

use_cases.title: Corporate and workplace nutrition programs
use_cases.description: Support in-house nutrition teams delivering employee wellness and nutrition benefits at scale.
use_cases.audience: Corporate wellness and HR benefits teams
